It runs between the Indonesian island of Sumatra to the west and peninsular (West) Malaysia and extreme southern Thailand to the east. So, pair III is correctly matched. It links the Indian and Pacific oceans and is one of the busiest and most important shipping lanes in the world. Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia control the joint patrolling of the Malacca Strait. The Strait of Taiwan lies between Taiwan and Mainland China. It connects the East China Sea and the South China Sea. So, pair IV is not correctly matched. So, only three of the above rows are correctly matched. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of major global straits and the water bodies they connect.

questionText:
Consider the following islands:
I. Andaman and Nicobar
II. Maldives
III. Seychelles
IV. Marshall
How many of the islands given above are coral islands?
explanation: Coral reefs are marine structures formed by the calcium carbonate skeletons of tiny living organisms called coral polyps. These reefs thrive in shallow, warm, and clear tropical waters, typically between 30°N and 30°S latitudes. While the Andaman and Nicobar Islands have both fringing and barrier reefs, they are not classified as corral islands. They are the extension of the Arakan Yoma range of Myanmar. The West coast of the Andaman Islands has the barrier reef, which is recognised among the other barrier reefs like those of New Caledonia, Belize, Fiji and Western Australia. These barrier reefs protect the islands from strong ocean waves and support diverse marine life. So, point I is not correct. The Maldives consist of a chain of coral atolls formed upon the submerged peaks of an ancient volcanic mountain range. The islands are low-lying, with barrier reefs surrounding the atolls providing natural protection against the erosive impact of monsoons. So, point II is correct. The Seychelles islands, located in the Indian Ocean, are characterized mainly by fringing reefs surrounding their granitic islands. These reefs grow close to shorelines and are rich in marine biodiversity. So, point III is correct. The Marshall Islands in the Pacific Ocean consist almost entirely of atolls and coral islands. Like the Maldives, they formed from submerged volcanoes surrounded by coral reefs, creating ring-shaped lagoons. The Atolls of the Marshall Islands together make up more than 10 percent of all the atolls in the world. So, point IV is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of coral reefs, their types, and their geographical distribution.

questionText: Which one of the following countries has the largest Mica reserves in the world?
explanation: India’s mica reserves are among the largest in the world, and are of a better grade than those found in other countries with large reserves, such as China and Brazil. Mica is mainly used in the electrical and electronic industries because it can be split into very thin sheets that are tough and flexible. India has a near monopoly in the production of mica, producing about 60 per cent of the world's total production. In India, mica is primarily produced in Jharkhand, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana and Rajasthan, followed by Tamil Nadu, West Bengal and Madhya Pradesh. In Jharkhand, high-quality mica occurs in a 150 km long and 22 km wide belt in the Lower Hazaribagh Plateau. In Andhra Pradesh, Nellore district produces the best quality mica. In Rajasthan, the mica belt extends about 320 km from Jaipur to Bhilwara and around Udaipur. Mica deposits are also found in Mysuru and Hassan districts of Karnataka, Coimbatore, Tiruchirappalli, Madurai and Kanniyakumari in Tamil Nadu, Alleppey in Kerala, Ratnagiri in Maharashtra, and Purulia and Bankura districts in West Bengal. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of mineral resources, specifically mica, its uses, and distribution in India.

questionText:
Consider the following information:
Crop Type Largest Producing State
I. Cotton Rabi Maharashtra
II. Gram Rabi Madhya Pradesh
III. Maize Kharif Karnataka
IV. Soyabean Kharif Andhra Pradesh
In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
explanation: Agriculture in India is largely divided into Kharif (monsoon) and Rabi (winter) seasons. Kharif crops are sown with the onset of the southwest monsoon (June–July) and harvested in October–November, while Rabi crops are sown in winter (October–December) and harvested in spring (March–April). Cotton is a Kharif crop, not a Rabi crop. It requires high temperatures and a long frost-free period, thriving in black soil regions. Major producers include Gujarat, Maharashtra, Telangana, and Madhya Pradesh. Gujarat leads in production (as per Economic Survey 2024–25). So, row I is not correctly matched. Gram (chana) is a Rabi pulse crop, grown in the winter season under residual soil moisture. Madhya Pradesh is the largest producer of gram, followed by Maharashtra and Rajasthan. So, row II is correctly matched. Chickpea (Gram), Lentils, Peas, Pigeon Pea, Kulthi (Horse Gram), Masoor (Red Lentil), Lobias (Lobia Beans) are the pulses which are grown in the rabi season. Green Gram (Moong), Black Gram (Urad), and Pigeon Pea (Toor) are grown during the kharif season. Maize (corn) is mainly a Kharif crop, though some areas also grow it in Rabi. The largest producing state is Karnataka, followed by Bihar and Madhya Pradesh (Economic Survey 2024–25). So, row III is correctly matched. Soybean, along with groundnut, is a Kharif oilseed crop and requires warm temperatures and moderate rainfall. Madhya Pradesh is the largest producer, contributing around 50% of India’s total production, followed by Maharashtra and Rajasthan. So, row IV is not correctly matched. Mustard, Linseed, and Sesame are grown in the rabi season. Rice, Maize, Sorghum (Jowar), Bajra (Pearl Millet) are examples of kharif crops, whereas Wheat, Barley, Oats are the rabi crops. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of Indian cropping seasons and the classification of major crops.

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
City Located on the bank of
I. Machilipatnam Godavari
II. Hampi Tungabhadra
III. Thanjavur Krishna
IV. Vidisha Betwa
V. Gandhinagar Sabarmati
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation: Machilipatnam is a port city located on the bank of the River Krishna in the Krishna district of Andhra Pradesh. So, pair I is not correctly matched. The 'Hampi' is located on the banks of the Tungabhadra River. So, pair II is correctly matched. Thanjavur is situated in the Cauvery delta. So, pair III is not correctly matched. Vidisha is located on the bank of the Betwa river. So, pair IV is correctly matched. Gandhinagar lies on the west bank of the Sabarmati river. So, pair V is correctly matched . Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of Indian cities and the rivers they are situated on.

questionText: Recently, Cyclone Montha made its landfall along which coast of India?
explanation: Cyclone Montha was a Severe Cyclonic Storm that formed over the west-central Bay of Bengal (BoB) as a low-pressure system. It made landfall in coastal Andhra Pradesh, causing heavy rainfall, strong winds, and extensive damage across Andhra Pradesh, Odisha, and parts of Tamil Nadu. So, statement I is correct. The cyclone was named by Thailand under the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) – Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (ESCAP) Panel on Tropical Cyclones (PTC). The word ‘Montha’ in Thai means a beautiful or fragrant flower. So, statement II is not correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of recent tropical cyclones, their impact areas, and naming conventions.

questionText:
What is the correct sequence of the following cities according to their latitudinal distance from the Tropic of Cancer, starting from the nearest to the farthest?
I. Udaipur
II. Kolkata
III. Delhi
IV. Aizawl
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: The Tropic of Cancer, located at approximately 23°27′ N latitude, marks the northernmost point on Earth where the Sun can appear directly overhead. This latitude corresponds to the Sun’s maximum northern declination relative to the celestial equator. Each year, around June 21—the summer solstice in the Northern Hemisphere—the Sun reaches this maximum declination and is positioned directly above the Tropic of Cancer. Coordinates of cities: Aizwal 23.7307° N, 92.7173° E, Kolkata: 22.5744° N, 88.3629° E, Udaipur: 24.5854° N, 73.7125° E, Delhi: 28.7041° N, 77.1025° E. So, the correct sequence of the cities according to their latitudinal distance from the Tropic of Cancer, starting from the nearest to the farthest is: Aizawl, Kolkata, Udaipur and Delhi. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of the Tropic of Cancer and the latitudinal coordinates of major Indian cities.

questionText:
With reference to Makhana (Foxnut), consider the following statements:
I. It is native to the Southeast Asian region.
II. It is usually cultivated in still or stagnant water bodies such as ponds, lakes, and swamps.
III. India contributes more than three-fourths of the global production of Makhana.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Makhana, also known as foxnut or gorgon nut (Euryale ferox), is an important aquatic cash crop cultivated in still or stagnant water bodies such as ponds, lakes, swamps, and oxbow lakes. Traditionally rooted in the Mithila region of Bihar, it holds cultural, nutritional, and economic significance. So, statement II is correct. It is considered native to South-East Asia and China, though it is cultivated across tropical and subtropical regions of the world. Historically, its use as both food and medicine dates back to ancient China and later spread across India, Japan, Malaysia, Thailand, and Nepal. So, statement I is correct. Makhana thrives in tropical and subtropical climates, requiring temperatures between 20°C and 35°C, relative humidity of 50–90%, and annual rainfall of 100–250 cm. It grows best in smooth loamy soils under stagnant perennial water bodies. Bihar, West Bengal, Assam, Manipur, Tripura, and Uttar Pradesh provide ideal agro-climatic conditions for its cultivation. India today dominates global Makhana production, contributing over 90% of the total world output. Bihar alone accounts for more than 80% of India’s domestic production, followed by smaller outputs from West Bengal, Assam, and Uttar Pradesh. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of specific agricultural crops, their cultivation requirements, and production statistics.

questionText:
With reference to the jet streams, consider the following statements:
I. These are the high-velocity, geostrophic air streams that flow in a narrow band around the Earth.
II. They generally flow in the upper levels of the troposphere.
III. When the temperature contrast between air masses is maximum, the jet stream tends to flow in a nearly straight path.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Jet streams are upper tropospheric, high-velocity, geostrophic winds formed due to steep pressure and temperature gradients between air masses, particularly along the polar front. Their speeds generally range between 110–185 km/h, but can exceed 300 km/h in stronger systems. So, statement I is correct. They play a crucial role in influencing global weather systems, including the movement of cyclones, western disturbances, and the onset of monsoons. The main jet streams include the subtropical westerly jet and the polar front jet in both hemispheres. The temperature contrast between adjoining air masses (e.g., tropical and polar air) determines the strength and alignment of jet streams. When this contrast is maximum, especially during winter, the jet stream’s velocity is highest and it flows in a nearly straight zonal path. Conversely, when the contrast weakens, the jet stream becomes meandering or wavy, forming Rossby waves. So, statement III is correct. Jet streams are generally found in the upper levels of the troposphere, right below the tropopause. So, statement II is correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of atmospheric circulation, specifically jet streams and their characteristics.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to natural gas:
I. It is mainly a mixture of methane with small amounts of ethane and other hydrocarbons.
II. More than 50% of India’s natural gas production comes from offshore fields.
III. Gujarat is the largest onshore producer of natural gas in India.
IV. India has set a target to increase the share of natural gas in the energy mix to 15% by 2030.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Natural gas is an important fossil fuel because it can be easily transported through pipelines.It is a hydrocarbon mixture mainly composed of saturated light paraffins such as methane and ethane, which remain gaseous under atmospheric conditions. It may also contain propane, butane, pentane, and hexane, and even these heavier hydrocarbons stay gaseous in reservoirs because of high pressure. So, statement I is correct. Natural gas production in India increased by 5.1% in 2023, marking the third consecutive year of growth. Total production reached over 1.2 trillion cubic feet (Tcf), the highest level since 2012. During FY 2022–23, offshore fields contributed 68% of India’s natural gas output, while onshore fields accounted for 32%. So, statement II is correct. Assam was the largest onshore producer, contributing nearly one-third of onshore natural gas production, followed by Rajasthan. So, statement III is not correct. The Government has set a target to increase the share of natural gas in India’s energy mix from about 6% at present to 15% by 2030. So, statement IV is correct. It is stored under high pressure as Compressed Natural Gas (CNG), which is used for power generation and as a cleaner fuel for transport vehicles, since it causes less pollution. Natural gas also serves as an input in the manufacture of chemicals and fertilizers. India has significant reserves of natural gas, and major deposits are located in Tripura, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, and the Krishna-Godavari delta. Mumbai High is India’s largest offshore oil and natural gas field. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of energy resources, specifically natural gas, its composition, and production in India.

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Protected Area River Flowing Through It
I. Panna National Park Betwa
II. Gandhi Sagar Wildlife Sanctuary Chambal
III. Bhitarkanika National Park Baitarani
IV. Nameri Tiger Reserve Barak
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation: Panna National Park and Tiger Reserve is located in Vindhya Hill in Madhya Pradesh. It is the land of the Ken River. The main forest here is Deciduous forest, mainly teak forest & Teak-Kardhai mixed forest. Panna Tiger Reserve was included in the global network of biosphere reserves by the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation (UNESCO) in 2020. So, pair I is not correctly matched. Ken flows through Panna Tiger Reserve. It flows through two states, namely Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh. The river is the last tributary of the Yamuna before the Yamuna joins the Ganga. It originates on the north-west slopes of the Kaimur Range. It is known for its rare Sajhar or Dendritic Agate stone. The Gandhi Sagar Wildlife Sanctuary is spread across western Madhya Pradesh. It features a diverse ecosystem of savanna, open grasslands, dry deciduous forests, and riverine areas. It is recognized as an Important Bird and Biodiversity Area (IBA). River Chambal bisects the Sanctuary into almost two equal halves and portions of the southern side is bounded by the reservoir of the Gandhisagar dam. So, pair II is correctly matched. Chambal River rises in the Vindhya Range just south of Mhow, western Madhya Pradesh. The river forms the boundary between Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh before joining the Yamuna in Uttar Pradesh. Bhitarkanika National Park is located in Odisha. It is India’s second-largest mangrove ecosystem after the Sunderbans. Bhitarkanika has one of the largest populations of endangered saltwater crocodiles in India. It is the second Ramsar site of the State after the Chilika Lake. It is known for the visit of Olive Ridley turtles for mass nesting. Bhitarkanika National Park is inundated by the rivers Baitarani, Brahmani, and Dhamara. Bhitarkanika forms the deltaic region of the river Brahmani and Baitarani. So, pair III is correctly matched. Baitarani originates from the Gonasika Hills in Odisha. The Nameri National Park and Tiger Reserve is situated in Assam, along the foothills of Arunachal Pradesh. It constitutes the core of Nameri Tiger Reserve. It lies on the north bank landscape of the River Brahmaputra. Nameri National Park shares its boundary with Pakhui Wildlife Sanctuary in Arunachal Pradesh. Nameri Tiger Reserve is inundated by rivers Jia-Bhoreli (also known as Kameng) in the west and Bor-Dikorai in the east. Jia-Bhoreli is a tributary of the Brahmaputra River. Bor-dikorai river is a tributary of Jia Bhoreli. The Barak River does not flow through Nameri National Park. So, pair IV is not correctly matched. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of Indian national parks, their geographical locations, and the rivers flowing through them.

questionText:
With regard to cobalt, consider the following statements:
I. The Democratic Republic of Congo is the largest producer of cobalt in the world.
II. India has 100% import dependency for cobalt.
III. Due to its magnetic properties, cobalt is used in making powerful magnets.
IV. Cobalt-60 can be used to kill bacteria and sterilize fresh food.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Cobalt is a hard, lustrous, silver-grey metal that is used mainly as a cathode material in lithium-ion and other types of batteries. It is primarily mined as a by-product of copper or nickel mining. It is also used in powerful magnets, cutting tools and high-strength alloys in the aerospace, energy and defense sectors. The Democratic Republic of Congo is the world's largest mined cobalt producer, contributing 73.0% of global output. Because of the rapid expansion of its nickel industry, Indonesia has recently emerged as the second-largest cobalt producer, accounting for 7.3% of global production. So, statement I is correct. Australia produces almost half of the world’s lithium, is the second-largest producer of cobalt and the fourth-largest producer of rare earths. India currently imports 100% of its lithium and cobalt requirements, which are essential minerals for electric vehicle battery manufacturing. China, Belgium, the Netherlands, the US, and Japan are the major suppliers of cobalt to India. So, statement II is correct. Cobalt has magnetic properties, being a ferromagnetic metal; it can attract and be magnetized by powerful magnets. It is used to create powerful, permanent magnets for applications such as motors, speakers, and medical equipment. So, statement III is correct. Cobalt compounds have been used since antiquity as a pigment for pottery, glass, paints and other media. It plays a crucial role in human nutrition as a component of vitamin B12. Gamma radiation from a radioactive source such as cobalt-60 can be used to kill bacteria on fresh food and enable it to keep for longer. So, statement IV is correct. Cobalt-60 is a radioactive isotope that serves as a significant source of gamma radiation, widely utilized in the medical field for radiotherapy in cancer treatment and for the sterilization of medical equipment and supplies. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of mineral resources, specifically cobalt, its uses, and global production.

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Mountain Type of Plate Boundary
I. Rockies Continent - Continent
II. Alps Oceanic - Continent
III. Taurus Oceaninc- Oceaninc
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation: The Rocky Mountains of North America were primarily formed due to oceanic–continental convergence, not continent–continent collision. The North American Plate, a major continental plate, has moved westward, whereas the Juan de Fuca Plate—a minor oceanic plate—and the Pacific Plate—a major oceanic plate—have drifted eastward. These mountains constitute an extensive range extending from Canada to central New Mexico. So, pair I is not correctly matched. The Alps were formed due to the collision of two continental plates — the African Plate and the Eurasian Plate. This continent–continent convergence led to intense folding, faulting, and uplift, creating the Alpine mountain system. So, pair II is not correctly matched. Intense tectonic folding associated with this orogenic event uplifted marine sedimentary rocks through processes of thrusting and folding, giving rise to prominent mountain peaks such as Mont Blanc (4,810 m) along the French–Italian border. The Taurus Mountains of southern Turkey were formed due to the collision between the African and Eurasian tectonic plates, which is a continent–continent convergence. The compression and uplift resulting from this collision created the Taurus range. So, pair III is not correctly matched. The mountains form an extensive mountain chain running roughly parallel to the Mediterranean coast. This system extends in a curved alignment from Lake Eğirdir in the west to the upper reaches of the Euphrates River in the east. So, none of the above pairs are correctly matched. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of plate tectonics and the formation of major mountain ranges.

questionText:
Consider the following information:
Ocean current Type Location
I. Leeuwin Warm Western coast of Australia
II. Agulhas Cold Western coast of South Africa
III. Oyashio Warm Eastern Coast of Japan
IV. Canary Cold Western Coast of North Africa
In which of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
explanation: Ocean currents are the continuous flow of a huge amount of water in a definite direction. Ocean currents are like river flow in oceans. They represent a regular volume of water in a definite path and direction. Ocean currents can also be classified based on temperature: as cold currents and warm currents. The Leeuwin Current is a warm ocean current that flows southward along the western coast of Australia. It originates from tropical waters near Indonesia and moves down past Cape Leeuwin, influencing the climate of Western Australia by bringing warm, low-salinity water. This warm current contributes to milder coastal temperatures, supports coral reef ecosystems, and influences rainfall patterns. So, pair I is correctly matched. The Agulhas Current is actually a warm ocean current, not a cold one. It flows southward along the eastern coast of South Africa and Mozambique, forming the western boundary current of the Indian Ocean. It carries warm tropical water from the Indian Ocean and meets the cold Benguela Current near the Cape of Good Hope, creating zones of upwelling and rich marine biodiversity. So, pair II is not correctly matched. The Agulhas leakage, an inflow of warm, salty water from the Indian Ocean into the South Atlantic, is a crucial component of the global ocean overturning circulation. This exchange can influence the thermohaline property in the North Atlantic and potentially modulate the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) variability on interannual to millennial timescales. The Oyashio Current is a cold subarctic current, not a hot one. It flows southward along the eastern coast of Japan, bringing cold, nutrient-rich waters from the Bering Sea and the Arctic Ocean. It meets the warm Kuroshio Current near the northeastern coast of Japan, creating upwelling zones that are some of the world’s richest fishing grounds. So, pair III is not correctly matched. The Canary Current is a cold ocean current flowing southward along the western coast of North Africa from Spain and Portugal to Senegal. It forms part of the North Atlantic Gyre. The North Atlantic Current divides into two branches in the eastern Atlantic: the North Atlantic Drift, which continues northeast toward the British Isles and Norway as the Norwegian Current, and the southern branch, which flows between Spain and the Azores to form the Canary Current. The latter eventually joins the North Equatorial Current, completing the North Atlantic circulation. Within this gyre lies the Sargasso Sea, noted for its dense mats of Sargassum seaweed and unique oceanographic characteristics. The Canary Current causes upwelling of nutrient-rich waters, making regions like the Canary Islands and Moroccan coast highly productive fishing zones. So, pair IV is correctly matched. So, only two of the above pairs are correctly matched. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of global ocean currents, their temperatures, and geographical locations.

questionText:
With reference to the Adiabatic Lapse Rate (ALR), consider the following statements:
I. The Wet ALR is mainly associated with unstable atmospheric conditions.
II. A positive ALR releases latent heat of condensation which is a major energy source for the development of thunderstorms and cyclones.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Adiabatic Lapse Rate (ALR) refers to the rate of temperature change in a rising or sinking air parcel without any heat exchange with the environment. Rising air expands and cools adiabatically, while sinking air compresses and warms. The Wet (Moist) Adiabatic Lapse Rate (WALR) applies to saturated air parcels, where condensation releases latent heat, reducing the rate of cooling to around 4°C/km. High moisture content, active condensation, and latent heat release create instability, allowing air parcels to continue rising. Thus, WALR is strongly associated with unstable conditions, favouring cloud formation, thunderstorms, and deep convection. So, statement I is correct. A positive ALR means temperature decreases with height, which is essential for a rising air parcel to cool adiabatically. As the parcel rises, expansion-induced cooling leads to condensation, releasing latent heat of condensation—the major energy source for thunderstorms, tornadoes, and tropical cyclones. This rising, cooling, and latent-heat–driven process is the first and fundamental step in generating severe convective weather systems. So, statement II is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of atmospheric thermodynamics, specifically adiabatic lapse rates and their role in weather systems.

questionText:
Which of the following are the primary commercial sources of Lithium in the world?
I. Salt lakes
II. Pegmatite rocks
III. Clay and borate minerals
IV. Deep-sea manganese nodules
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: Lithium is mixed (alloyed) with aluminium and magnesium for light-weight alloys, and is also used in batteries, some greases, some glasses, and in medicine. It does not occur as the free metal in nature because of its high reactivity. Deposits are known all around the world. Salt lakes (salars): Lithium-rich brines from salt lakes constitute a major commercial source of lithium, particularly in South America. So, point I is correct. Pegmatite rocks: Hard-rock mining of lithium-bearing pegmatites is another important commercial source, with Australia being the leading producer. So, point II is correct. Clay and borate minerals: These occur in sedimentary basins and represent commercially viable or emerging sources of lithium. So, point III is correct. Deep-sea manganese nodules: Although these nodules contain lithium and are considered a potential future resource, they are not presently exploited commercially due to technological, logistical, and environmental constraints. So, point IV is not correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of mineral resources, specifically lithium, its sources, and uses.

questionText:
Consider the following rivers:
I. Subarnarekha
II. Vaigai
III. Vaitarna
IV. Shetruniji
Which of the rivers given above drains into the Bay of Bengal?
explanation: The drainage system of India is broadly classified based on the direction in which rivers flow either into the Arabian Sea or into the Bay of Bengal. Approximately 77% of India’s total drainage area flows into the Bay of Bengal, while the remaining 23% drains into the Arabian Sea. The Subarnarekha is one of the longest east-flowing inter-state rivers. TIt rises near Nagri village in the Ranchi District of Jharkhand. It drains into the Bay of Bengal. Its basin extends over Jharkhand, Odisha and West Bengal. Its principal tributaries are the Kanchi, the Karkari and the Kharkai. So, point I is correct. The Vaigai River is an important river of Tamil Nadu. The river Vaigai originates in the eastern slope of the Western Ghats mountainous offshoot in the Varushanad area. Thereafter it flows east and southeast directions till it confluences with the Bay of Bengal. The Suruliyar and the Manjalar are its important tributaries. So, point II is correct. The Vaitarna River rises from the Trimbak hills in the Nasik district of Maharashtra. It is a west-flowing river. It flows through Maharashtra and finally drains into the Arabian Sea. So, point III is not correct. The Shetruniji is one of the major rivers of Gujarat. It rises at Chachai hills in Gir Forest of Gujarat. It flows towards the east and empties into the Arabian Sea through the Gulf of Cambay. So, point IV is not correct. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of Indian river systems and their drainage directions.

questionText:
With reference to Primary Rocks, consider the following statements:
I. These rocks are unfossiliferous.
II. These are formed by lithification and cover 75 per cent of the Earth’s crust.
III. Granite is a primary rock.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Primary rocks, also known as igneous rocks, are the earliest-formed rocks of the Earth’s crust and serve as the foundation for all other rock types. They originated from the cooling and solidification of molten magma — either deep beneath the Earth’s surface (intrusive) or on the surface after volcanic eruptions (extrusive). These rocks provide the parent material from which sedimentary and metamorphic rocks are derived. Since primary (igneous) rocks form from the solidification of molten magma, the extremely high temperatures during their formation destroy any organic matter. As a result, fossils are very rare in these rocks. This is why igneous rocks such as basalt, granite, and gabbro are termed unfossiliferous. So, statement I is correct. Lithification, the compaction and cementation of sediments, is the process by which sedimentary rocks are formed. Although sedimentary rocks cover about 75% of the Earth’s surface, igneous and metamorphic rocks constitute the majority of the Earth’s crust by volume. So, statement II is not correct. Granite is a classic example of an intrusive igneous rock, which forms when magma cools slowly beneath the Earth’s surface, allowing large crystals to develop. It is termed acidic and contains minerals such as quartz, feldspar, and mica. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of rock types, their formation processes, and characteristics.

questionText:
With regard to the Terai soil in India, consider the following statements:
I. It is rich in nitrogen content.
II. It is poorly drained and marshy soil, occurring in a narrow belt south of the Bhabar region.
III. It is suitable for the cultivation of jute and sugarcane.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The alluvial soils are divided into old alluvium (Bhangar), new alluvium (Khadar), Bhabar, and Terai. These soils are pale in colour and sandy, while being less kankary in nature. Due to the softness of the strata and fertility, the alluvial soils are well suited to irrigation and respond very well to canal and well/tube-well irrigation. If properly maintained, these soils can yield splendid crops of rice, wheat, sugarcane, tobacco, cotton, jute, maize, oilseeds, vegetables, and fruits. Along the Shiwalik foothills, there is a zone 8–16 km wide known as Bhabar. To the south of the Bhabar, running parallel to it, lies a long narrow strip of swampy lowland with silty soils called the Terai. It is poorly drained and marshy in nature, occurring in a narrow belt south of the Bhabar region. So, statement II is correct. The soils of this region are rich in nitrogen and organic matter but deficient in phosphate. So, statement I is correct. These soils are suitable for a number of crops such as wheat, rice, sugarcane, jute, and soybean under reclaimed conditions. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of soil types in India, specifically alluvial soils and their subdivisions.

questionText: Which of the following statements best describes a Rip Current?
options: (a) A narrow current of water flowing parallel to the shoreline, caused by longshore drift
(b) A strong, localized and fast-moving current of water flowing directly away from the shore
(c) A vertical underwater current that brings cold, nutrient-rich water from deeper oceanic layers to the surface
(d) A deep ocean current caused by differences in temperature and salinity
explanation: A Rip Current is a strong, narrow, and fast-moving stream of water that flows seaward — from the shoreline back toward the open ocean, sea, or lake. These currents are among the most well-known and dangerous coastal hazards faced by swimmers and surfers worldwide. Rip currents are formed primarily due to variations in beach topography such as sandbars, piers, reefs, or underwater ridges. When waves break onshore, water accumulates near the beach and must return seaward. If this returning water finds a narrow gap — between sandbars or around obstacles — it rushes back toward the sea as a concentrated, high-speed current called a rip current. Contrary to popular belief, rip currents do not pull people underwater; rather, they carry them away from the shore, often beyond the breaking waves. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of coastal oceanography, specifically the formation and characteristics of rip currents.

questionText: With reference to the physiography of India, ‘Didwana’, ‘Kuchaman’ and ‘Pachpadra’ are the names of:
explanation: Didwana is a wetland located in Rajasthan. It is a natural inland saline lake. Didwana lake is the second largest saltwater lake in Rajasthan. Migratory birds from North Asia and Siberia, including flamingos, falcons and other winter avifauna, gather at Didwana lake every year. Kuchaman lake is a continental saline lake. It is located in Rajasthan. The present status of the lake is that of a playa. Pachpadra Lake is an important saltwater lake located near the town of Pachpadra in Rajasthan, India. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of Indian lakes, specifically saline lakes and their locations.

questionText:
With reference to coal in India, consider the following statements:
I. India has more than one-fourth of the world’s coal reserves.
II. It generally has a low sulphur content.
III. It has the largest share in India’s power generation.
IV. Gondwana coal accounts for the largest reserve and production in India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Coal is one of the principal fossil fuels and serves as a primary source of energy and power. The term coal is generally applied to a sedimentary formation that is highly carbonaceous in nature and is formed by the accumulation of previously existing plant material. Indian coal has low sulphur content, which helps reduce sulphur-related emissions. So, statement II is correct. The ash content in Indian coal typically ranges from 15% to 45%, with most of the domestic production falling in the 35%–45% category. In contrast, imported coal typically has an ash content of 10%–20%. The higher ash percentage in India’s coal is primarily due to its drift origin. India has the fifth-largest coal reserves globally and is the world's second-largest consumer of coal. It has around ten percent of the world’s coal reserves. So, statement I is not correct. By 2024, Jharkhand remained India’s leading coal-producing state, accounting for approximately 26% of the nation’s total coal reserves, estimated at around 28 billion tonnes. Located in eastern India, Jharkhand’s extensive coal deposits play a pivotal role in sustaining the country’s energy sector. Odisha, holding about 25.1% of India’s coal reserves, is similarly resource-rich, with major deposits in the Talcher and Ib Valley coalfields. Chhattisgarh, with an 18.1% share, is emerging as a significant contributor through the rapid development of its Korba, Raigarh, and Surguja coalfields, reinforcing its strategic importance in India’s coal industry. Coal remains crucial, contributing 55% to the national energy mix and fuelling over 74% of total power generation. So, statement III is correct. Gondwana coal accounts for approximately 98% of India’s total coal reserves and nearly 99% of its domestic coal production. So, statement IV is correct. The Gondwana coal, which is about 250 million years old, has lower carbon content as compared to Carboniferous coal (350 million years old), which is almost absent in India due to geological reasons. Gondwana coalfields produce both coking and non-coking coal, and include bituminous as well as sub-bituminous grades. Anthracite deposits are generally absent in Gondwana formations. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of energy resources, specifically coal, its types, and distribution in India.

questionText:
With reference to the Himalayan mountain system, consider the following statements:
I. The Shiwaliks are more continuous and widespread than the Himadri.
II. The Eastern Himalayas, on average, have a higher elevation than the Western Himalayas.
Which of the statements given above is/are not correct?
explanation: The Himalayan range is a young fold mountain. These mountain ranges run in a west-east direction from the Indus to the Brahmaputra. Himalaya comprises a chain of parallel mountain ranges. The three main ranges are: The Greater Himalayas are the northernmost range known as the Himadri. It bears some of the highest mountain peaks (ranging from 6000 to 8000 m), including Mount Everest. It is the most continuous range. The average height of this range from sea level is about 6100 metres. The Lesser Himalaya or Himachal lies to the south of the Greater Himalaya. The breadth of the Himachal range is 60 to 80 km, and the height varies from 1000 metres to 4500 metres. The Pir Panjal, Dhauladhar, Nag Tibba and Mahabharat Range are common high ranges of the Lesser Himalaya. Also known as the Outer Himalayas, the Shiwalik Range forms the southernmost and geologically youngest section of the Himalayan mountain system. It lies between the Great Plains and the Lesser Himalayas, extending for about 2,400 km from the Potwar Plateau in Pakistan to the Brahmaputra Valley in Assam. The range has an altitude of 600–1,500 metres and a width varying from about 50 km in Himachal Pradesh to less than 15 km in Arunachal Pradesh. The southern slopes are steep, while the northern slopes are gentle. The Shiwaliks form an almost continuous chain of low hills, except for a gap of about 80–90 km occupied by the Tista and Raidak river valleys. Forest cover is dense in the eastern and central sections (Nepal and Northeast India) but decreases westward due to declining rainfall. In Punjab and Himachal Pradesh, the southern slopes are nearly barren and are dissected by seasonal streams known as Chos. Geologically, the Shiwaliks consist of unconsolidated sediments such as sand, clay, and conglomerates, deposited by rivers from the Himalayas. Himadri is the only Himalaya range that maintains its continuity from west to east. It is more continuous and widespread than the Shiwaliks. So, statement I is not correct. The Western Himalayas, on average, have a higher elevation than the Eastern Himalayas. Also, the altitudinal variations are greater in the eastern half than in the western half. So, statement II is not correct. In the western Himalayan ranges, elevation increases gradually as the mountains rise from the plains in a series of successive stages. Consequently, the higher mountain ranges are situated farther away from the plains. In contrast, the eastern Himalayan ranges ascend abruptly from the plains, bringing the high peaks, such as Kanchenjunga, relatively close to the plains. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires detailed knowledge of the Himalayan mountain system and its various ranges.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I:
Tropical and subtropical coastal deserts are primarily influenced by ocean currents.
Statement II:
In lower latitudes, warm currents along eastern ocean shores contribute to the formation of coastal deserts.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: (a) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
(b) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
(c) Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
(d) Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
explanation: Ocean currents are large-scale movements of surface water driven by winds, Earth’s rotation, and differences in water temperature and salinity. They play a crucial role in redistributing heat across the globe and thus greatly influence the climate of coastal regions. Generally, warm currents flow from the equator toward the poles, while cold currents move from polar to tropical regions. This pattern affects rainfall, temperature, and even the formation of deserts along continental coasts. Deserts like the Atacama (South America), Namib (Africa), and Sahara (Africa) lie along the west coasts of continents. They are influenced by cold currents (e.g., Humboldt, Benguela currents and Canary current), not warm ones. Cold currents lower evaporation and suppress rainfall, leading to arid conditions. So, statement I is correct. Warm currents like the Brazil Current, Agulhas Current, and Kuroshio Current flow along the eastern coasts of continents in tropical and subtropical zones, bringing warm and humid conditions. So, statement II is not correct. So, statement–I is correct but Statement–II is incorrect. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of ocean currents and their influence on global climate and desert formation.

questionText:
In the context of cloud-seeding, consider the following statements:
I. It works on cirrus clouds only.
II. Silver iodide is the most widely used chemical for cold cloud seeding.
III. It requires sufficient moisture content in the clouds to succeed.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Cloud seeding is a weather-modification technique that introduces artificial nuclei into suitable clouds to enhance precipitation. It is used globally for drought mitigation, increasing snowpack, or occasionally for pollution management. The technique requires moisture-laden clouds, appropriate temperatures, and specific chemical nuclei. Its success is highly variable and dependent on atmospheric conditions. Cloud seeding is broadly of two types, cold cloud seeding and warm cloud seeding. Cold cloud seeding targets subfreezing clouds containing supercooled water droplets. Silver iodide (AgI) is most widely used because its crystal lattice closely resembles natural ice, enabling instant freezing and ice-crystal growth. Other agents include dry ice (solid CO₂) and liquid propane. So, statement II is correct. Warm cloud seeding primarily uses hygroscopic salts like sodium chloride and calcium chloride. These salts attract water molecules, forming large droplets that grow and fall as rain in clouds with temperatures above freezing. Cloud seeding does not work on all cloud types, but the limitation is not restricted only to cirrus clouds. Clouds may be unsuitable for seeding depending on moisture availability, temperature, vertical development, and cloud dynamics. Only certain types, typically cumulus, stratiform, and suitable supercooled clouds, respond effectively to seeding. So, statement I is not correct. The cloud-seeding attempt in Delhi failed mainly due to a lack of adequate moisture in the clouds. For seeding to succeed, clouds must contain sufficient water vapour or supercooled droplets. IIT-Kanpur confirmed that although particulate concentration reduced slightly, rainfall could not occur because the moisture levels were too low, preventing nucleation and subsequent raindrop growth. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of weather modification techniques, specifically cloud seeding and its requirements.

questionText:
With reference to the Doldrums (Equatorial Low-Pressure Belt), consider the following statements:
I. It is characterized by extremely low pressure throughout the year.
II. Only horizontal air currents are found in this region.
III. It has zero coriolis force, preventing the formation of tropical cyclones.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The Doldrums, also known as the Equatorial Low-Pressure Belt or the Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ), extend roughly between 10°N and 10°S, though the width may vary with the Sun’s apparent movement. Intense vertical heating near the equator causes strong convection, producing a zone of permanent low pressure, weak surface winds, high humidity, and frequent thunderstorms. This belt experiences extremely low pressure because the equatorial region receives maximum insolation, causing the air to heat rapidly, expand, and rise. Persistent convection keeps surface pressure low throughout the year. Therefore, the doldrum zone is characterized by permanent low pressure. So, statement I is correct. Winds from both hemispheres converge in this region and begin to rise sharply, leaving no significant horizontal (surface) wind movement. As a result, the area is known for calm conditions, lack of steady winds, and the dominance of vertical currents. This is precisely why sailors historically feared this zone for its windless conditions. So, statement II is not correct. At the equator, the Coriolis force approaches zero due to the Earth’s geometry. With no deflective force to initiate rotation, tropical cyclones cannot form in the Doldrums, despite high temperatures and abundant moisture. This is why the equatorial belt, though ideal for convection, is cyclone-free. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of global atmospheric circulation, specifically the Doldrums and their characteristics.

questionText:
With reference to ravines, consider the following statements:
I. They are deep, narrow gorges that are generally larger than a gully.
II. They are formed primarily by lateral corrasion of mature rivers in their plain course.
III. The Luni Basin and the Yamuna Basin in India exhibit significant badland or ravine topography.
Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
explanation: Running water is one of the most powerful agents of erosion that shapes the Earth’s surface over time. As streams and rivers flow, they erode soil and rocks, creating a variety of erosional features that differ in size, depth, and intensity of erosion. A ravine is a deep, narrow gorge formed by prolonged erosion and represents an advanced stage of gully erosion, making it generally larger than a gully. So, statement I is correct. Ravines are formed mainly by vertical erosion and headward cutting due to surface runoff and flash floods. Lateral corrasion is characteristic of mature rivers in their plain course and is not the typical process responsible for ravine formation. So, statement II is not correct. The Yamuna Basin (notably the Chambal–Yamuna region) and the Luni Basin in Rajasthan exhibit extensive badland or ravine topography caused by intense surface erosion of soft, poorly consolidated soils under semi-arid conditions. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of fluvial geomorphology, specifically erosional features like ravines and gullies.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. India is the world's third-largest producer of solar-generated electricity.
II. India has surpassed the 50% mark for non-fossil fuel sources in its total installed electricity capacity.
III. Gujarat is the largest producer of solar power in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are not correct?
explanation: As one of the fastest-growing economies, India has set ambitious targets to ensure energy security, promote environmental health and meet its global climate commitments under the Paris Agreement. This includes a pledge to achieve 500 gigawatts (GW) of renewable capacity by 2030 and reach net-zero carbon emissions by 2070. India has become the world’s third-largest solar energy producer with an installed capacity of about 125 GW, as stated by the Minister for New and Renewable Energy. So, statement I is correct. India’s renewable energy targets are ambitious, with a strategic approach to harness its abundant solar resources. By 2030, India aims to reach 280 GW of solar power, which will form a significant portion of the country’s overall target of 500 GW of renewable energy. India has achieved one of its NDCs which was to achieve about 50% of the cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy resources by 2030. So, statement II is correct. India’s total installed power capacity is now majority non-fossil fuel based. Non-fossil fuel sources, including renewable energy, hydro, and nuclear, account for 256.09 GW, which is over 51% of the country’s total capacity. Solar power has reached 127.33 GW, while fossil-fuel-based sources indicate rapid growth in clean energy deployment. As of late 2025, India’s solar power landscape shows Rajasthan maintaining its lead, followed by Gujarat, with Maharashtra having risen to the third position. So, statement III is not correct. The Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan, with a capacity of 2.2 GW, is the third largest solar park worldwide, highlighting India’s commitment to scaling its renewable infrastructure. The International Solar Alliance (ISA) launched by India and France in 2015 at COP21 now has 124 member and signatory countries, and continues to guide the global solar agenda through climate finance, technology, policy and skill development. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of India's renewable energy targets, achievements, and solar power infrastructure.

questionText:
Arrange the following hills of Peninsular India in the correct sequence of their location from North to South:
I. Shevaroy
II. Kudremukh
III. Anaimudi
IV. Nallamala
V. Doda Betta
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: Shevaroy Hills are located in the Eastern Ghats. The Shevaroy Hills are located in the Salem district of Tamil Nadu. Kudremukh Peak is located in the Western Ghats in the Chikmagalur district of Karnataka. It has a marked similarity to the face of a horse. It is a part of the Kudremukh Range. Anaimudi peak (2695 m) is situated in Kerala. It is the highest peak of the Anaimalai hills, which is a part of the Western Ghats. Nallamalla hills are in Andhra Pradesh. It is a part of the Eastern Ghats. Doda Betta (2,637 m) is the second-highest peak of South India. It is situated in the Nilgiri hills. Doddabetta is the highest Peak in Tamil Nadu. As can be seen from the image given above, the correct sequence of the given hills as one moves from North to South is: Nallamala Hills - Kudremukh Hills - Shevaroy Hills - Doda Betta - Anaimudi. So, the correct answer is IV- II- I- V- III. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires geographical knowledge of the hill ranges in South India and their north-to-south arrangement.

questionText:
With reference to the temperature distribution of oceans, consider the following statements:
I. In the Arctic and Antarctic regions, a distinct thermocline layer is present throughout the year.
II. Enclosed seas, both in lower and higher latitudes, tend to have relatively higher temperatures compared to open oceans.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Ocean temperature exhibits both horizontal variation with latitude and vertical variation with depth. The surface layer receives direct solar insolation, making it relatively warm, while the deeper layers remain progressively colder. The transition zone between these layers, characterized by a rapid decrease in temperature with increasing depth, is known as the thermocline. In the Arctic and Antarctic circles, surface water temperatures are already close to 0°C, leaving little temperature difference between surface and deep waters. As a result, no distinct thermocline forms in these polar regions. The water column is nearly uniformly cold from the surface to the ocean floor, forming a single cold layer instead of three layers (surface, thermocline, deep water) that are observed in tropical and mid-latitude oceans. So, statement I is not correct. The temperature of enclosed seas depends on their latitude and the degree of mixing with open ocean waters. In low latitudes (tropical regions), enclosed seas (like the Red Sea or Persian Gulf) do record higher temperatures than nearby open oceans because of restricted circulation, high evaporation, and intense solar radiation. However, in high latitudes (polar or subpolar regions), enclosed seas (like the Baltic Sea) actually record lower temperatures than open oceans. This occurs because they are surrounded by cold landmasses and have limited exchange with warmer ocean currents. So, statement II is not correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of ocean temperature profiles, thermoclines, and the thermal characteristics of enclosed seas.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Oort Cloud:
I. It is a cloud of dust that surrounds the Solar System.
II. It begins beyond the Kuiper Belt, marking the outermost region of the Solar System.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: In 1950, astronomer Jan Oort proposed that some comets originate from a vast, extremely distant spherical shell of icy bodies (not a cloud of dust) surrounding the solar system. This enormous region of icy objects is now known as the Oort Cloud, which occupies space at distances between 5,000 and 100,000 astronomical units (AU) from the Sun. So, statement I is not correct. It is a distant and dynamic region of the Solar System that overlaps with and extends beyond the Kuiper Belt. So, statement II is correct. The leading theory suggests the Oort Cloud formed 4.6 billion years ago when planets finished forming and many planetesimals remained. Planetary gravity, especially that of Jupiter, scattered these icy objects. Some were ejected from the solar system, while others were pushed into very distant, eccentric orbits still controlled by the Sun. Objects in the Oort cloud do not share a common orbital plane, can travel above, below, and at various inclinations around the Sun and form a thick bubble of distant icy debris. The Oort Cloud may contain hundreds of billions to trillions of icy bodies. When disturbed, one of these objects begins a long journey toward the Sun, becoming a long-period comet. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of the solar system's outer regions, specifically the Oort Cloud and its characteristics.

questionText:
With reference to the Polar Vortex, consider the following statements:
I. It is a circumpolar low-pressure system found at both of the Earth’s poles.
II. In the Northern Hemisphere, it is a counter-clockwise flow of air that is associated with Rossby waves.
II. It can lead to ozone depletion over the polar regions during winter.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The polar vortex is a large, circumpolar, upper-tropospheric to lower-stratospheric low-pressure system composed of extremely cold air. It forms over the both the poles during winter and is closely linked with the polar jet stream. So, statement I is correct. The polar vortex refers to the counter-clockwise (cyclonic) circulation of air in the Northern Hemisphere (and clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere) caused by the Coriolis effect, which helps confine cold air near the poles. Disturbances or meanders in the polar vortex and the associated jet stream are linked to atmospheric Rossby waves. So, statement II is correct. The polar vortex plays a crucial role in ozone depletion, especially over Antarctica. During polar winters, extremely cold temperatures lead to the formation of Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs) within the vortex. These clouds convert chlorine-containing reservoir compounds into highly reactive radicals (Cl, ClO), which rapidly destroy ozone with the return of sunlight. The ozone hole is therefore most intense when the polar vortex is strong and temperatures remain below −78°C, favoring PSC formation. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of atmospheric circulation, specifically the polar vortex and its role in ozone depletion.

questionText:
With reference to rock-forming minerals, consider the following statements:
I. Amphibole forms nearly half of the Earth's crust.
II. Cinnabar is typically found in areas of geologically recent volcanic activity, commonly associated with geysers and fumaroles.
III. Granitic pegmatites are the most common and economically important source of high-quality industrial feldspar.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Rock-forming minerals are naturally occurring inorganic substances that constitute the majority of Earth’s crust. They play key roles in various geological processes and also have significant industrial and economic importance. Common rock-forming minerals include silicates like feldspar, quartz, and amphibole, and non-silicates like calcite and hematite. Amphibole is a group of minerals that occur in metamorphic and igneous rocks. However, it does not form nearly half of the Earth's crust. The majority of the Earth's crust is composed of feldspar (around 50%) and quartz (about 12%), followed by pyroxene, mica, and amphibole in smaller amounts. So, statement I is not correct. Cinnabar (mercury sulphide) typically occurs as a vein-filling mineral in regions of geologically recent volcanic activity and is commonly deposited by alkaline hot springs, geysers, and fumaroles. So, statement II is correct. Cinnabar (HgS, mercury sulfide) is the chief ore of mercury. When heated, cinnabar releases mercury vapor, which is then condensed to obtain liquid mercury. Although feldspar is widely distributed, granitic pegmatites constitute the most important commercial source, as they yield high-purity, large-crystal feldspar required for glass and ceramic industries. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of rock-forming minerals, their abundance in the Earth's crust, and their economic significance.

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. Burhi Gandak River joins the Ganga River in the Patna district of Bihar.
II. Kosi is a transboundary river that flows through India and Nepal only.
III. The Mahananda River originates in the Darjeeling hills and joins the Ganga River in Bangladesh.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Burhi Gandak River is a significant tributary of the Ganga River. It is a vital water source in Bihar. It flows parallel to the Gandak River. Burhi Gandak joins Ganga from the left near Khagaria. So, statement I is not correct. The Burhi Gandak is also known as ‘Sikrahna’ in Bihar. It is one of the tributaries that completely flows in Bihar. It originates from Chautarwa Chaur near Bishambharpur in the district of West Champaran. Burhi Gandak passes through the districts of West Champaran, East Champaran, Muzaffarpur, Samastipur, and Khagaria. The last district of Burhi Gandak is Khagaria, where it joins the River Ganga near Gogri Jamalpur. Kosi is a transboundary river that flows through China, Nepal, and India. The Kosi River, an antecedent river, originates north of Mount Everest in Tibet, where its main stream, the Arun, rises. In Nepal, it is joined by the Sun Kosi from the west and the Tamur Kosi from the east, forming the Sapt Kosi. The river then flows into India, joining the Ganga on its left bank near Kursela. So, statement II is not correct. Mahananda is a typical river system consisting of two different streams, one arising from the mountainous region of the Himalayas in Nepal, traverses through the Indian state of Bihar and out falls in the Ganga in the left opposite to Rajmahal in Bihar and is locally named as the river Fulahar. The other stream, also named as Mahananda, rises downhill in Darjeeling in West Bengal and traverses a distance of about 400 kms. through the district of Darjeeling, West Dinajpur and Malda and enters in Bangladesh territory and ultimately out falls in the Ganga near Godagarighat in Bangladesh, just opposite to Lalgola in Murshidabad district of West Bengal. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of the Ganga river system and its major tributaries.

questionText:
With reference to the Dams/Hydro power projects in India, consider the following information:
Dam/ Hydroelectric Project Constructed on the River Located In
I. Ranjit Sagar Beas Punjab
II. Baglihar Jhelum Jammu and Kashmir
III. Almatti Krishna Karnataka
IV. Mullaperiyar Periyar Kerala
In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
explanation: The Ranjit Sagar Dam, also known as the Thien Dam, is constructed on the river Ravi. It is located in Punjab. The dam is a part of the hydroelectricity project of the state Government of Punjab and was completed in 2001. So, pair I is not correctly matched. Baglihar Dam is a run-of-the-river power project on the Chenab River. The dam, which was commissioned in 2009, is operated by the J&K Power Development Corporation. Baglihar Stage-1 has an installed capacity of 450 megawatts. So, pair II is not correctly matched. Almatti Dam is a hydroelectric project on the Krishna River in Karnataka. It is the main reservoir of the Upper Krishna Irrigation Project. It was built with the primary objectives of supplying irrigation and potable water to adjacent regions, generating hydroelectric energy, and managing flood risks in the area. So, pair III is correctly matched. The Mullaperiyar dam was built in 1895 across the Periyar River in Kerala's Idukki district. It is operated by Tamil Nadu under a lease agreement. It has long been a point of contention with Kerala, citing safety concerns due to its age and seismic vulnerability. Tamil Nadu emphasises its importance for irrigation and drinking water supply to several southern districts. It is one of the oldest dams in India. Its construction began in 1887 and was completed in 1895. So, pair IV is correctly matched. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of major dams in India, the rivers they are built on, and their locations.

questionText:
With reference to the west-flowing rivers in India, consider the following statements:
I. The Tapti River drains into the Arabian Sea through the Gulf of Kutchh.
III. The Mahi River drains into the Arabian Sea through the Gulf of Khambhat.
III. The Mandovi River falls into the Karanji Lake.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Tapti is an important westward flowing river. It originates from Multai in the Betul district of Madhya Pradesh. It is 724 km long and drains an area of 65,145 sq. km. Nearly 79 per cent of its basin lies in Maharashtra, 15 per cent in Madhya Pradesh and the remaining 6 per cent in Gujarat. Its important tributaries are the Suki, the Gomai, the Arunavati and the Aner which joins it from right, and those joining from left are the Vaghur, the Amravati, the Buray, the Panjhra, the Bori, the Girna, the Purna, the Mona and the Sipna. The river falls into the Arabian Sea through the Gulf of Cambay or Khmabat. So, statement I is not correct. Mahi River originates from Vindhyachal Hills in Madhya Pradesh and meets in Gulf of Khambhat. The Mahi basin extends over states of Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan and Gujarat. The total length of Mahi is 583 km. The river finally falls into the Gulf of Khambhat in the Arabian Sea. So, statement II is correct. The Mandovi River is one of the main west-flowing rivers of Goa. It rises in the Jamboti Ghats and falls into the Arabian Sea. So, statement III is not correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of west-flowing rivers in India, their origins, and drainage basins.

questionText:
With reference to the region of South East Asia, consider the following statements:
I. The Philippine Island Arc was formed due to the convergence of the Philippine Sea Plate and the Eurasian Plate.
II. The Phu Khanh Basin is an important sedimentary basin owned by Thailand.
III. The Strait of Singapore is bordered by Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Philippine Island Arc is an example of a convergent plate boundary. It has formed where the Philippine Sea Plate subducts beneath the Eurasian Plate. This tectonic interaction gives rise to a chain of volcanic islands and deep-sea trenches. So, statement I is correct. The Phu Khanh Basin is a north-to-south elongated basin in the Vietnam continental shelf area, which comprises a series of Cenozoic sedimentary basins, located within a transitional zone from the continental crust of the Indochina Craton to the oceanic crust of the South China Sea. So, statement II is not correct. The Strait of Singapore is a narrow and busy maritime passage that connects the Strait of Malacca (to the west) with the South China Sea (to the east). It is bordered by Singapore to the north, Indonesia to the south, and Malaysia to the northwest. This strait is one of the world’s most important shipping lanes, facilitating trade between the Indian Ocean and the Pacific Ocean. So, statement III is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of global tectonic features, sedimentary basins, and important maritime straits.

questionText:
With reference to the formation of dew, consider the following statements:
I. It forms under conditions of clear skies, calm air, high relative humidity, and long, cold nights.
II. The Dew point of the air must be above the freezing point.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Dew is formed when water vapour near the ground condenses into liquid droplets on exposed surfaces as they cool to their dew point temperature. Ideal conditions for dew formation include clear skies (allowing strong radiational cooling), calm or light winds (preventing mixing of air layers), high relative humidity (providing ample moisture), and long, cold nights (giving enough time for surfaces to cool to the dew point). These conditions enable water vapour to condense into droplets on grass, metal surfaces, and soil. So, statement I is correct. Dew forms only when the dew point is above 0°C. When the dew point falls below freezing, water vapour does not condense into liquid; instead, it sublimates directly into ice crystals, producing frost, not dew. Thus, dew formation becomes impossible under sub-freezing dew point conditions. So, statement II is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of atmospheric condensation processes and the conditions necessary for dew formation.

questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding Tidal Bores:
I. A tidal bore is a surge of seawater that travels upstream in a river when the incoming tide opposes the river flow.
II. Tidal bores occur frequently along all coasts having a narrow river outlet, irrespective of the tidal range.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: A tidal bore represents a positive surge of water created when a rising ocean tide pushes into the mouth of a river, moving upstream against the natural river flow. This usually occurs during spring tides or periods of supermoons, when tidal forces are strongest. The bore often appears as a wall of water traveling inland. Examples include the Qiantang River bore in China and the Pororoca on the Amazon River. So, statement I is correct. Tidal bores do not occur along all coasts, even those with narrow river outlets. Their formation requires a specific set of conditions: A large tidal range (usually greater than 6 meters). A funnel-shaped estuary—wide at the mouth and narrowing inland. A shallow river channel with suitable topography. The alignment of tidal timing with river flow conditions. Many rivers around the world do not meet these criteria, which is why tidal bores are a rare phenomenon, observed only in select locations. So, statement II is not correct. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of tidal phenomena, specifically tidal bores and the conditions required for their formation.

questionText: The terms ‘Chena’, ‘Roca’, ‘Ray’, and ‘Podu’ are used to refer to which one of the following practices?
explanation: Shifting cultivation, also called slash-and-burn agriculture, is a primitive subsistence farming system practiced on small patches of land using simple tools like hoe, a dao, and digging sticks, and relying mainly on family or community labour. It depends entirely on the monsoon, the natural fertility of the soil, and favourable environmental conditions. In this system, farmers cut, slash, and burn a forested patch, mix the ashes with soil, and grow crops such as maize, yam, potatoes, and cassava. When the soil fertility declines, the land is abandoned, and a new patch is cleared for cultivation. Since no fertilisers or modern inputs are used, productivity remains low. Traditionally, the fallow period ranged from 10 to 20 years, but in recent times it has reduced to 2–5 years, leading to soil degradation. Chena, Roca, Ray, and Podu are all various local names for shifting cultivation, also known as 'slash-and-burn' agriculture, practiced in different parts of the world. In India, this practice is known by different names — Jhum in Assam and other northeastern states, Punam Krishi in Kerala, Podu in Andhra Pradesh and Odisha, and Bewar, Mashan, Penda, and Beera in Madhya Pradesh. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of agricultural practices, specifically shifting cultivation, and its regional names.

questionText:
Consider the following species:
I. Great Hornbill
II. Sloth Bear
III. Lion-tailed Macaque
IV. Hangul
Which of the above species demonstrates a pronounced ecological dependence on evergreen forest ecosystems?
explanation: Evergreen forests are defined as dense, multilayered forests characterized by broad-leaved trees that retain their foliage year-round. They are typically found in regions where the climate is generally wet and the dry season is very brief. Great Hornbill is a large bird characterized by a curved yellow bill and a prominent hollow casque. It is native to South and Southeast Asia including India. It is listed as ‘Vulnerable’ by the IUCN and protected under Schedule I of the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972. In India, Great Hornbills are found in the Western Ghats and the Himalayas. It is the state bird of Arunachal Pradesh and Kerala. Great hornbills are found in evergreen and moist deciduous forests, primarily inhabiting the canopy of tall trees. For nesting, they prefer trees that tower above the canopy level. So, point I is correct. Sloth Bears is one of the four bear species found in India. Sloth bears live in India, Nepal and Sri Lanka. It is listed in the ‘vulnerable’ category by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN). While the sloth bear occurs across a variety of habitats including moist tropical forests it predominantly inhabits dry deciduous forests, scrublands, and grasslands across the Indian subcontinent. Its presence in wet evergreen forests is comparatively limited, and it does not exhibit a pronounced or primary ecological dependence on evergreen forest ecosystem. So, point II is not correct. Lion-tailed Macaque (Macaca silenus) is a primate endemic to rainforests of the Western Ghats. The Lion-tailed Macaque show a high frequency of feeding on fruits (59.46 %). The lion-tailed macaques are mostly seen in taller trees. So, point III is correct. Hangul or Kashmiri Stag is a unique subspecies of red deer native to the Kashmir region. It is the only surviving Asiatic member of the red deer family. It is the state animal of Jammu and Kashmir (UT). The hangul is classified as Critically Endangered by the IUCN Red List. Hangul’s population have constantly declined over the decades, making it largely confined to the 141 square kilometres of Dachigam National Park. The hangul lives in dense riverine forests, high valleys and mountains of the Kashmir valley and northern Chamba in Himachal Pradesh. It prefers deciduous woodlands, natural grasslands, pastures and meadows, and sometimes ventures above the tree-line. It feeds mainly on grasses, herbs, leaves, bark and twigs. The hangul is native to the dense forests and high-altitude meadows of the Kashmir Valley. So, point IV is not correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires knowledge of Indian wildlife species and their specific habitat preferences.

questionText:
With reference to the eye of a tropical cyclone, consider the following statements:
I. The greater the wind speed in a tropical cyclone, the larger is the eye region.
II. It is the region of heaviest rainfall and strongest convection.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Tropical cyclones are intense low-pressure systems forming over warm ocean waters, characterized by strong rotating winds and heavy rainfall. Their structure consists of a calm central eye, surrounded by the eyewall with maximum winds and intense convection, and spiral rainbands extending outward. They derive energy from warm sea surface evaporation and latent heat release. The eye of a tropical cyclone is the central calm region surrounded by the eyewall, which contains the most intense winds, rainfall, and convective activity. The eye forms due to strong rotational forces causing sinking air in the center. As a cyclone intensifies, wind speeds increase and centripetal force strengthens, causing the eye to contract. Thus, stronger cyclones have smaller, tighter eyes (known as “pin-hole eyes”). Weaker cyclones have broader eyes. So, statement I is not correct. The eye is actually the calmest and warmest part of the cyclone, with clear skies and very little rainfall. The heaviest rainfall and strongest winds occur in the eyewall, the ring of deep convective clouds surrounding the eye. So, statement II is not correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
difficulty_rationale: Requires understanding of the structure and dynamics of tropical cyclones.

questionText: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the typical methane yield potential of the given feedstocks, arranged from highest to lowest?
options: (a) Fats and oils > Food waste > Cattle manure > Wheat straw
(b) Cattle manure > Wheat straw > Food waste > Fats and oils
(c) Wheat straw > Cattle manure > Fats and oils > Food waste
(d) Food waste > Fats and oils > Wheat straw > Cattle manure
explanation: Methane yield from different biomass feedstocks depends largely on their energy density and chemical composition, particularly the ease with which their organic matter can be decomposed by anaerobic microorganisms during biogas production. Fats and Oils (Lipids): These are the most energy-dense organic materials and generate the highest methane yield per unit mass, as they are readily broken down under anaerobic conditions. Food Waste (mixed carbohydrates, proteins, and fats): Food waste generally shows a high methane potential due to the presence of easily degradable carbohydrates and proteins, along with some fats. Cattle Manure: Although available in large quantities, manure has already undergone partial digestion in animals, reducing the amount of readily available organic energy and thus its methane yield. Wheat Straw (lignocellulosic material): Dominated by cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, with lignin being highly resistant to microbial action, wheat straw yields the lowest methane output among these feedstocks. So, the correct sequence representing the typical methane yield potential of the given feedstocks, arranged from highest to lowest is: Fats and oils > Food waste > Cattle manure > Wheat straw. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: Geography
